typography.title: Typography for dyslexia
typography.summary: Larger size, increased line spacing, and a clean sans-serif font reduce visual strain and the rate of fixation errors. Wider character and word spacing further help by reducing visual crowding — the tendency for adjacent letters to interfere with each other in the reader's peripheral vision.
citations: British Dyslexia Association (2023). Dyslexia Style Guide.
Rello, L. & Baeza-Yates, R. (2013). Good fonts for dyslexia. ASSETS '13.

letter-spacing.title: Increased letter spacing
letter-spacing.summary: A randomized study with dyslexic children found that increasing letter spacing improved reading accuracy by ~20% and reading speed without any training — the effect was attributed to reduced visual crowding.
citations: Zorzi, M. et al. (2012). Extra-large letter spacing improves reading in dyslexia. PNAS, 109(28).

theme.title: Colour themes and background
theme.summary: Pure white backgrounds increase glare for many dyslexic readers; warm off-whites (cream, beige) and dark themes are widely preferred. Coloured tinted overlays have a long history but research is mixed — they help some readers and not others, so we offer them as a user preference rather than a clinical claim.
citations: British Dyslexia Association (2023). Dyslexia Style Guide.
Henderson, L. M., Tsogka, N. & Snowling, M. J. (2013). Questioning the benefits that coloured overlays can have for reading in students with and without dyslexia. JORSEN, 13(1).
theme.note: Frame coloured backgrounds as a personal-comfort setting, not a treatment.

speech.title: Text-to-speech with word highlighting
speech.summary: A meta-analysis of 22 studies found that TTS — especially when paired with synchronized visual highlighting of the spoken word — produced a moderate positive effect on reading comprehension for struggling readers (d ≈ 0.35).
citations: Wood, S. G., Moxley, J. H., Tighe, E. L., & Wagner, R. K. (2017). Does use of text-to-speech and related read-aloud tools improve reading comprehension for students with reading disabilities? A meta-analysis. Journal of Learning Disabilities.

ruler.title: Reading ruler / line focus
ruler.summary: Dyslexic readers often show less stable binocular fixation, which makes it easy to skip or repeat lines. A reading ruler — a horizontal highlight, dimming, or spotlight — gives the eye a clear anchor and reduces regressions.
citations: Bhattacharya, S. et al. (2023). Digital reading rulers for dyslexic readers. ACM CHI.
Bucci, M. P., Brémond-Gignac, D. & Kapoula, Z. (2011). Latency of saccades and vergence eye movements in dyslexic children. Experimental Brain Research.

bionic.title: Bionic Reading
bionic.summary: Bolding the first portion of each word creates 'fixation anchors' — high-contrast targets the eye can land on as it moves along the line. The technique was popularised by a 2017 Swiss app of the same name and has spread widely in dyslexia and ADHD communities online. Subjective reports from dyslexic readers are often positive, but controlled eye-tracking and comprehension studies have not produced consistent gains, so we expose it as a personal-preference mode rather than a treatment.
citations: Casutt, R. (2017). Bionic Reading — original concept. bionic-reading.com
Multiple independent reading-research groups have published mixed empirical findings (2022–2024).
bionic.note: Try it on a passage you find tiring. If the bolded anchors help your eye flow, leave it on; if the alternating weight feels choppy, switch it off.

syllables.title: Click-to-split syllables
syllables.summary: Phonological processing — the ability to break words into smaller sound units — is the strongest predictor of reading difficulty in dyslexia. Structured Literacy explicitly teaches readers to decode long words by breaking them into syllables, and on-demand splitting supports that habit during independent reading. Implementation note: we use Liang's hyphenation algorithm via the Hypher library, which closely matches true English syllabification (~90%).
citations: International Dyslexia Association. Structured Literacy: An Introduction.
Snowling, M. J. (2000). Dyslexia (2nd ed.). Blackwell.
Goswami, U. (2015). Sensory theories of developmental dyslexia: three challenges for research. Nature Reviews Neuroscience.

ai-simplify.title: AI-powered plain-language rewriting
ai-simplify.summary: Complex vocabulary and convoluted sentences are a documented barrier for many dyslexic readers, beyond what typography alone can fix. The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) and W3C Cognitive Accessibility Task Force both list plain-language alternatives as a primary accommodation. This feature applies plain-language principles (short sentences, common words, active voice) at the press of a button. It is a tool that applies the principles — not a treatment.
citations: W3C Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.1 — Success Criterion 3.1.5 (Reading Level).
W3C Cognitive Accessibility Task Force. Making Content Usable for People with Cognitive and Learning Disabilities.
ai-simplify.note: Always verify AI-rewritten text against the original for important documents.
